title: Loading Shared Libraries
text: Loading Shared Libraries Shared libraries can be loaded statically at startup or dynamically at runtime. Static Loading Static loading occurs at startup, if the executable is explicitly linked with (dependent on) a specific shared library. For example, when you start up the Uniface program, the operating system will immediately load the Uniface core shared library ucall , because that shared library was linked with the executable. Note that linking a shared library to an executable does not mean that all the code in that library is 'glued' to the executable, as is the case with a link using relocatable libraries. Instead, the executable merely contains the names of the shared libraries so that the operating system loader knows that these libraries must be immediately loaded at startup time. We will refer to this method as static loading. Static loading never uses the full name of a DLL as found in the executable. This would not work, because that name is the one that was used by Uniface when building the executable, and this will almost never match the name on a customer’s system. Instead, the operating system just uses the base name of the shared library, and then uses an environment variable (the search path) to look up the shared library. This environment variable is usually a colon- or semicolon- separated list of directories, which the operating system will try from left to right until it finds the shared library with the requested name. The name of this environment variable depends on the platform: On iSeries (AS/400) there is no comparable mechanism. For more information, see 3GL Compile and Link Commands . Table 1. Platform environment variables Platform Environment variable Windows PATH AIX LIBPATH Other Unix, Linux LD_LIBRARY_PATH On Windows, the PATH variable is set in the System Properties section of the Control Panel. On Unix and Linux, the variable needs to be set in the shell, and then exported using the export command. You can see occurring in the file adm/insunis that you must always execute before running Uniface. This file puts the Uniface lib directory in the relevant environment variable, so your Uniface application can successfully load all the required shared libraries. Dynamic Loading Dynamic loading occurs at runtime, when Uniface decides it must call some specific code. For example, if your application first accesses the U6.1 Oracle connector, Uniface will dynamically load the shared library uora61 (the exact name is platform-dependent), locate the connector entry point, and finally call that code in the shared library. The same happens when your application calls 3GL code as specified in the [USER_3GL] section in the assignment file. We refer to this method as dynamic loading. Dynamic loading also uses the search path environment variable, unless a full path name is specified in the [USER_3GL] section. For reasons of portability, we recommend that you not use full path names in the [USER_3GL] section. Whenever shared libraries need to be loaded, whether statically or dynamically, you should ensure that your search path is set correctly. (This does not apply to iSeries systems). It is a mistake to think that all shared libraries will be loaded from the same location as the executable or the libraries that we used in the link command. Make sure your search path contains the correct bin directory as the first entry. For example, suppose you have linked a Uniface 8.4 call-in application, and that you have both Uniface 8.3 and Uniface 8.4 installed on your system. If the Uniface 8.3 directory comes first in your search path, your Uniface 8.4 call-in application might load some of its shared libraries from the 8.4 bin directory, and others from the 8.3 bin directory. Because shared libraries are not compatible across Uniface versions, this is likely to lead to unexpected results. A similar problem can arise when loading a database connector. For example, loading the uora61 shared library might fail with unexpected errors when the search path does not contain the correct Oracle bin path. This is especially important if you have multiple versions of Oracle installed on your system, or if you make the wrong choice between 32-bits and 64-bits Oracle libraries. If this is not configured correctly, unpredictable results can happen. A good tool for Windows to spot this loading of incompatible DLLs is Filemon, a freeware tool that can be downloaded from www.sysinternals.com . If you see different Uniface path names being used, you can be sure there is a problem with the PATH variable. Another way to determine this could be to run your program within Dependency Walker, a freeware tool which can be downloaded from www.dependencywalker.com . On Unix platforms, problems like these can often be diagnosed by running the program under the truss command, if that tool is available on your Unix platform. For information, consult the man page for truss on your Unix system. When you create a shared library for use with Uniface, we recommend that you put it in the same directory as Uniface’s own shared libraries ( bin on Windows, lib on Unix and Linux). That way, it will always be found, without the need to modify the search path. Related Links Shared Libraries A shared library is a library of compiled 3GL code that is built in such a way that it can be loaded at run time, the code (though not the data) being shared by different applications. This is in contrast with static linking of relocatable code in the executable, where each process had its own copy of the code. Compiling and Linking 3GL How you build your 3GL code depends on the purpose of the code. [USER_3GL] Use the [USER_3GL] section of the assignment file to lists third-party components and Uniface 3GL extensions to be made available to Uniface. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
